retrieved_docs.title: Canavan Disease -- Evaluation -- Neuroimaging
retrieved_docs.content: MRI features include a relatively enlarged brain, also called megalencephaly. There is typically a diffuse bilateral white matter involvement. Unlike other leukodystrophies, subcortical U-fibers are usually involved early in the disease process. T1 shows a low signal in the white matter while T2 shows a high signal in white matter. There is no post contrast enhancement. MR spectroscopy shows markedly elevated levels of NAA, and an elevated NAA:creatine ratio. The mnemonic "CaNAAvan" is useful for remembering this information.

retrieved_docs.title: Canavan Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Macrocephaly, or enlarged head size, becomes prominent by the age of three to six months. After that, hypotonia progresses to spasticity, hyperreflexia, extensor plantar responses, and tonic extensor spasms. The extensor spasms may occur in response to noise. By the age of six months, neurologic irregularities are invariably present with little or no subsequent motor development.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of metabolic megalencephaly, the brain parenchyma is increased in size due to the deposition of metabolic products within the brain tissue. This can include the leukodystrophies such as Alexander disease, lysosomal storage diseases such as Tay-Sach disease, and organic acid disorders. Patients with these conditions often have a normal head circumference that is normal at birth but increases at a rate that exceeds their expected curve. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Canavan Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal form is the most common and is usually associated with the most severe symptoms. Most infants appear healthy immediately after birth, but within 2 to 6 months, obvious deficits in gross motor development will be apparent. The infant may not be able to move, turn over, control head movements, or support itself. Hypotonia is a common feature and is usually associated with macrocephaly. Most infants have difficulty with feeding and develop seizures. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: If the patient is developmentally appropriate for the age and has a normal neurologic exam, there are no abnormal features to suggest a specific genetic syndrome, and there is no family history of any developmental or neurologic problems, then Weaver curves may be utilized using both parents' head circumferences. If the patient is within normal range after plotting on this curve, then no further evaluation is needed. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Canavan Disease -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies
retrieved_docs.content: In symptomatic infants with compatible clinical features and neuroimaging findings suggesting aspartoacylase deficiency, the diagnosis is concluded by observing elevated levels of urine NAA. Compatible clinical features include hypotonia, poor head control, and macrocephaly. A determination can be reached more specifically by detecting deficient aspartoacylase activity in cultured skin fibroblasts. If elevated urine NAA and skin fibroblast testing are diagnostic, genetic testing is obtained only for genetic counseling. [7] [8] [9] Urine levels of NAA are increased up to 200 times that of the reference range. Gas chromatography or mass spectrometry are employed for measurement.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Benign familial macrocephaly is not associated with any complications. However, macrocephaly due to hydrocephalus can have serious complications, including death, if not promptly treated. Other complications associated with macrocephaly are seizures, developmental disabilities, and neurologic deficits.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Macrocephaly is the condition in which the head circumference of an infant is above 2 standard deviations, which is above the 97th percentile. It can be due to benign conditions or can be due to underlying causes that lead to serious sequelae like neurological deficits and developmental delays. It is, therefore, imperative to understand the different etiologies of macrocephaly and to be able to refer infants who require further investigations and close monitoring. This activity outlines and describes the etiology, evaluation, and management of macrocephaly and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating infants and children with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of macrocephaly is determined by the underlying cause.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on the findings on history and physical, referrals to developmental therapies, pediatric neurology, genetics, or neurosurgery may be warranted. Good communication during any of these transfers of care is crucial for the continuity of care of the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to understand that there are different etiologies of macrocephaly. This understanding is needed to differentiate benign macrocephaly from conditions that require acute investigation and intervention to prevent long term neurological deficits and developmental delays.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: If present, an underlying cause or associated syndrome is important to be identified. However, benign familial macrocephaly as an isolated entity does not require significant workup and caregivers can be reassured.

retrieved_docs.title: Canavan Disease -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Intellectual disability Lack of motor skills Difficulty feeding Hypotonia Paralysis Blindness Seizures Death

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The other causes of anatomic megalencephaly are varied and often genetic. [2] Neurocutaneous disorders such as tuberous sclerosis, neurofibromatosis, and hypomelanosis of Ito may be associated with megalencephaly. Patients with autism spectrum disorder may have macrocephaly/megalencephaly. Achondroplasia may also present with megalencephaly or hydrocephalus.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Megalencephaly may be anatomic, which refers to an increase in the size or number of cells in the brain without metabolic disease process, or maybe metabolic. Anatomic megalencephaly is most often familial and benign. In these cases, the patient is often born with a large head and a normal-sized body with a further rapid increase in head size over the first six months of life (head circumference increases by 0.6 to 1 cm rather than average 0.4 cm/week). Familial megalencephaly usually produces mild macrocephaly, typically 2 to 4 cms above the 90th percentile, and the growth curve follows above but parallel to 98th percentile. Family members may also have large heads. [1]

Other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions are noted to use the condition described as diffuse encephalitis around the axis (الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور), linking inflammatory processes to genetic and degenerative disorders of the nervous system. Together, these relationships illustrate how hydrocephalus, its anatomical context, associated cognitive impairment, and various ventricular shunt procedures are interconnected within neuroanatomy, neurosurgical intervention, and disease classification, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2198
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary disorder of nervous system is a broad category of genetic conditions affecting the nervous system, and شقيقة فالجية عائلية (familial hemiplegic migraine) is a specific instance within this category. شقيقة فالجية عائلية represents a hereditary form of migraine that is characterized by transient hemiplegia and other neurological symptoms, illustrating how a hereditary disorder of the nervous system can manifest as a distinct migraine subtype.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2308
retrieved_docs.content: Other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions are linked to the presence of diffuse encephalitis around the axis, as indicated by الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور, suggesting that these conditions may involve or utilize this widespread inflammatory process in the brain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 167
retrieved_docs.content: (اضطراب المشية) in the context of cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy type 1. Sodium valproate (valproát sodný) has evidence supporting its use for migraine prophylaxis. Cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy type 1, illustrates the overlap between vascular cerebral disease, gait disorder, and migraine, emphasizing the interconnected nature of vascular, headache, and neurodegenerative processes. Overall, the community’s knowledge highlights the anatomical sites (brain, ventricles, nervous system), clinical associations (dementia, ischemic complications, cranial nerve dysfunction, gait disturbance), genetic and familial forms, and the role of neurosurgical procedures (extracranial ventricular shunting, nasopharyngeal diversion) and pharmacologic prevention (sodium valproate) in integrated diagnosis and management of these interrelated neurological conditions.
